name: followthemoney
documentation:
# Follow the Money

[![ftm-build](https://github.com/alephdata/followthemoney/actions/workflows/build.yml/badge.svg)](https://github.com/alephdata/followthemoney/actions/workflows/build.yml)

This repository contains a pragmatic data model for the entities most
commonly used in investigative reporting: people, companies, assets,
payments, court cases, etc.

The purpose of this is not to model reality in an ideal data model, but
rather to have a working data structure for researchers.

`followthemoney` also contains code used to validate and normalize many
of the elements of data, and to map tabular data into the model.

## Documentation

For a general introduction to `followthemoney`, check the high-level introduction:

* https://followthemoney.tech

Part of this package is a command-line tool that can be used to process and
transform data in various ways. You can find a tutorial here:

* https://followthemoney.tech/docs/cli/

Besides the introductions, there is also a full reference documentation for the
library and the contained ontology: 

* https://followthemoney.tech/explorer/

There's also a number of viewers for the RDF schema definitions generated
from FollowTheMoney, e.g.:

* [LODE documentation](http://150.146.207.114/lode/extract?url=https%3A%2F%2Falephdata.github.io%2Ffollowthemoney%2Fns%2Fftm.xml&owlapi=true&imported=true&lang=en)
* [WebVOWL](https://service.tib.eu/webvowl/#iri=https://alephdata.github.io/followthemoney/ns/ftm.xml)
* RDF/OWL specification in [XML](https://alephdata.github.io/followthemoney/ns/ftm.xml).

## Development environment

For local development with a virtualenv:

```bash
python3 -mvenv .env
source .env/bin/activate
pip install -e ".[dev]"
```

Now you can run the tests with

```bash
make test
```

## Releasing

We release a lot of version of `followthemoney` because even small changes
to the code base require a pypi release to begin being used in `aleph`. To
this end, here's the steps for making a release:

```bash
git pull --rebase
make build
make test
git add . && git commit -m "Updating translation files"
bumpversion patch
git push --atomic origin main $(git describe --tags --abbrev=0)
```

This will create a new patch release and upload a distribution of it. If
the changes are more significant, you can run `bumpversion` with the `minor`
or `major` arguments.

When the schema is updated, please update the docs, ideally including the
diagrams. For the RDF namespace and JavaScript version of the model, 
run `make generate`.

modules.name: tests.types.test_dates
documentation: -*- coding: utf-8 -*-

classes.name: followthemoney.types.ip.IpType

methods.name: followthemoney.types.ip.IpType.validate
documentation: Check to see if this is a valid ip address.

methods.name: followthemoney.types.ip.IpType.clean_text
documentation:
Create a more clean, but still user-facing version of an
instance of the type.

Internet protocol addresses. This supports both addresses used
by the protocol versions 4 (e.g. `192.168.1.143`) and 6
(e.g. `0:0:0:0:0:ffff:c0a8:18f`).

classes.name: followthemoney.types.string.StringType
documentation: A simple string property with no additional semantics.

classes.name: followthemoney.types.string.TextType
documentation:
Longer text fragments, such as descriptions or document text. Unlike
string properties, it might make sense to treat properties of this type as
full-text search material.

classes.name: followthemoney.types.string.HTMLType
documentation:
Properties that contain raw hypertext markup (HTML).

User interfaces rendering properties of this type need to take extreme
care not to allow attacks such as cross-site scripting. It is recommended
to perform server-side sanitisation, or to not render this property at all.

classes.name: followthemoney.types.country.CountryType

methods.name: followthemoney.types.country.CountryType.clean_text
documentation:
Determine a two-letter country code based on an input.

The input may be a country code, a country name, etc.

Properties to define countries and territories. This is completely
descriptive and needs to deal with data from many origins, so we support
a number of unusual and controversial designations (e.g. the Soviet Union,
Transnistria, Somaliland, Kosovo).

classes.name: followthemoney.types.gender.GenderType
documentation:
A human gender. This is not meant to be a comprehensive model of
the social realities of gender but a way to capture data from (mostly)
government databases and represent it in a way that can be used by
structured tools. I'm not sure this justifies the simplification.

modules.name: followthemoney.helpers
documentation: This module violates the boundary between the role of code and
YAML in the rest of followthemoney. It handles normalisations
which would be much harder to express in abstract, especially
those thet simplify the data based on their pragmatics.
If anyone were to swap out the default model, this would
probably be the first place to break.

functions.name: followthemoney.helpers.remove_checksums
documentation:
When accepting entities via a web API, it would consistute
a security risk to allow a user to submit checksum-type properties.
These can be traded in for access to said files if they exist in the
underlying content-addressed storage. It seems safest to just remove
all checksums from entities when they are untrusted user input.

functions.name: followthemoney.helpers.simplify_provenance
documentation:
If there are multiple dates given for some of the provenance
fields, we can logically conclude which one is the most meaningful.

functions.name: followthemoney.helpers.entity_filename
documentation: Derive a safe filename for the given entity.

functions.name: followthemoney.helpers.name_entity
documentation:
If an entity has multiple names, pick the most central one
and set all the others as aliases. This is awkward given that
names are not special and may not always be the caption.

functions.name: followthemoney.helpers.check_person_cutoff
documentation:
Check if a person has been dead long enough to not be relevant for
investigations any more.

functions.name: followthemoney.helpers.remove_prefix_dates
documentation:
If an entity has multiple values for a date field, you may
want to remove all those that are prefixes of others. For example,
if a Person has both a birthDate of 1990 and of 1990-05-01, we'd
want to drop the mention of 1990.

functions.name: followthemoney.helpers.remove_prefix_date_values
documentation: See `remove_prefix_dates`.

functions.name: followthemoney.helpers.inline_names
documentation:
Attempt to solve a weird UI problem. Imagine we are showing a list of
payments between a sender and a beneficiary to a user. They may now conduct
a search for a term present in the sender or recipient name, but there will
be no result, because the name is only indexed with the parties, but not in
the payment. This is part of a partial work-around to that.

This is really bad in theory, but really useful in practice. Shoot me.

functions.name: followthemoney.helpers.combine_names
documentation:
This function will try to build names from name parts provided as part
of a person entity. This is of course impossible to do culturally correctly
for the whole planet at once, so it should be mostly used for internal-facing
(e.g. matching) processes.

functions.name: followthemoney.helpers.dates_years
documentation: Get the unique years from a set of date strings.

functions.name: followthemoney.helpers.post_summary
documentation: Make a string summary for a Post object.

classes.name: followthemoney.types.language.LanguageType
documentation:
A human written language. This list is arbitrarily limited for some
weird upstream technical reasons, but we'll happily accept pull requests
for additional languages once there is a specific need for them to be
supported.

classes.name: followthemoney.types.url.UrlType

methods.name: followthemoney.types.url.UrlType.clean_text
documentation:
Perform intensive care on URLs to make sure they have a scheme
and a host name. If no scheme is given HTTP is assumed.

A uniform resource locator (URL). This will perform some normalisation
on the URL so that it's sure to be using valid encoding/quoting, and to
make sure the URL has a schema (e.g. `http`, `https`, ...).

classes.name: followthemoney.types.identifier.IdentifierType
documentation:
Used for registration numbers and other codes assigned by an authority
to identify an entity. This might include tax identifiers and statistical
codes.

Since identifiers are high-value criteria when comparing two entities, numbers
should only be modelled as identifiers if they are long enough to be meaningful.
Four- or five-digit industry classifiers create more noise than value.

classes.name: followthemoney.types.json.JsonType

methods.name: followthemoney.types.json.JsonType.pack
documentation: Encode a given value to JSON.

methods.name: followthemoney.types.json.JsonType.unpack
documentation: Decode a given JSON object.

methods.name: followthemoney.types.json.JsonType.join
documentation: Turn multiple values into a JSON array.

An encoded JSON object. This is used to store raw HTTP headers for documents
and some other edge cases. It's a really bad idea and we should try to get rid
of JSON properties.

classes.name: followthemoney.types.number.NumberType
documentation:
A numeric value, like the size of a piece of land, or the value of a
contract. Since all property values in FtM are strings, this is also a
string and there is no specified format (e.g. `1,000.00` vs. `1.000,00`).

In the future we might want to enable annotations for format, units, or
even to introduce a separate property type for monetary values.

classes.name: followthemoney.types.registry.Registry

methods.name: followthemoney.types.registry.Registry.add
documentation: Add a singleton class.

methods.name: followthemoney.types.registry.Registry.get
documentation:
For a given property type name, get its type object. This can also
be used via getattr, e.g. `registry.phone`.

methods.name: followthemoney.types.registry.Registry.get_types
documentation: Get a list of all type names.

This registry keeps the processing helpers for all property types
in the system. They are instantiated as singletons when the system is first
loaded. The registry can be used to get a type, which can itself then
clean, validate or format values of that type.

classes.name: followthemoney.types.entity.EntityType

methods.name: followthemoney.types.entity.EntityType.clean_text
documentation:
Specific types can apply their own cleaning routines here (this is called
by `clean` after the value has been converted to a string and null values
have been filtered).

A reference to another entity via its ID. This is how entities in FtM
become a graph: by pointing at each other using references.

Entity IDs can either be `namespaced` or `plain`, depending on the context.
When setting properties of this type, you can pass in an entity proxy or
dict of the entity, the ID will then be extracted and stored.

classes.name: followthemoney.types.name.NameType

methods.name: followthemoney.types.name.NameType.clean_text
documentation: Basic clean-up.

methods.name: followthemoney.types.name.NameType.pick
documentation: From a set of names, pick the most plausible user-facing one.

methods.name: followthemoney.types.name.NameType.compare
documentation: Compare two names for similarity.

A name used for a person or company. This is assumed to be as complete
a name as available - when a first name, family name or patronymic are given
separately, these are stored to string-type properties instead.

No validation rules apply, and things having multiple names must be considered
a perfectly ordinary case.

classes.name: followthemoney.types.email.EmailType

methods.name: followthemoney.types.email.EmailType.validate
documentation: Check to see if this is a valid email address.
def _check_exists(self, domain):
"""Actually try to resolve a domain name."""
try:
domain = domain.encode('idna').lower()
socket.getaddrinfo(domain, None)
return True
except:
return False

methods.name: followthemoney.types.email.EmailType.clean_text
documentation:
Parse and normalize an email address.

Returns None if this is not an email address.

methods.name: followthemoney.types.email.EmailType.rdf
documentation: def country_hint(self, value)
TODO: do we want to use TLDs as country evidence?

Internet mail address (e.g. user@example.com). These are notoriously hard
to validate, but we use an irresponsibly simple rule and hope for the best.

classes.name: followthemoney.types.common.PropertyType

methods.name: followthemoney.types.common.PropertyType.validate
documentation:
Returns a boolean to indicate if the given value is a valid instance of
the type.

methods.name: followthemoney.types.common.PropertyType.clean
documentation:
Create a clean version of a value of the type, suitable for storage
in an entity proxy.

methods.name: followthemoney.types.common.PropertyType.clean_text
documentation:
Specific types can apply their own cleaning routines here (this is called
by `clean` after the value has been converted to a string and null values
have been filtered).

methods.name: followthemoney.types.common.PropertyType.join
documentation:
Helper function for converting multi-valued FtM data into formats that
allow only a single value per field (e.g. CSV). This is not fully reversible
and should be used as a last option.

methods.name: followthemoney.types.common.PropertyType.specificity
documentation:
Return a score for how specific the given value is. This can be used as a
weighting factor in entity comparisons in order to rate matching property
values by how specific they are. For example: a longer address is considered
to be more specific than a short one, a full date more specific than just a
year number, etc.

methods.name: followthemoney.types.common.PropertyType.compare_safe
documentation: Compare, but support None values on either side of the comparison.

methods.name: followthemoney.types.common.PropertyType.compare
documentation:
Comparisons are a float between 0 and 1. They can assume
that the given data is cleaned, but not normalised.

methods.name: followthemoney.types.common.PropertyType.compare_sets
documentation: Compare two sets of values and select the highest-scored result.

methods.name: followthemoney.types.common.PropertyType.country_hint
documentation:
Determine if the given value allows us to infer a country that it may
be related to (e.g. using a country prefix on a phone number or IBAN).

methods.name: followthemoney.types.common.PropertyType.rdf
documentation:
Return an RDF term to represent the given value - either a string
literal, or a URI reference.

methods.name: followthemoney.types.common.PropertyType.pick
documentation: Pick the best value to show to the user.

methods.name: followthemoney.types.common.PropertyType.node_id
documentation:
Return an ID suitable to identify this entity as a typed node in a
graph representation of some FtM data. It's usually the same as the the
RDF form.

methods.name: followthemoney.types.common.PropertyType.node_id_safe
documentation: Wrapper for node_id to handle None values.

methods.name: followthemoney.types.common.PropertyType.caption
documentation:
Return a label for the given property value. This is often the same as the
value, but for types like countries or languages, it would return the label,
while other values like phone numbers can be formatted to be nicer to read.

methods.name: followthemoney.types.common.PropertyType.to_dict
documentation: Return a serialisable description of this data type.

class_variables.name: followthemoney.types.common.PropertyType.name
documentation: A machine-facing, variable safe name for the given type.

class_variables.name: followthemoney.types.common.PropertyType.group
documentation:
Groups are used to invert all the properties of an entity that have a
given type into a single list before indexing them. This way, in Aleph,
you can query for `countries:gb` instead of having to make a set of filters
like `properties.jurisdiction:gb OR properties.country:gb OR ...`.

class_variables.name: followthemoney.types.common.PropertyType.label
documentation: A name for this type to be shown to users.

class_variables.name: followthemoney.types.common.PropertyType.plural
documentation:
A plural name for this type which can be used in appropriate places in
a user interface.

class_variables.name: followthemoney.types.common.PropertyType.matchable
documentation:
Matchable types allow properties to be compared with each other in order to
assess entity similarity. While it makes sense to compare names, countries or
phone numbers, the same isn't true for raw JSON blobs or descriptive text
snippets.

class_variables.name: followthemoney.types.common.PropertyType.pivot
documentation:
Pivot property types are like a stronger form of {attr}`~matchable` types:
they will be used when value-based lookups are used to find commonalities
between entities. For example, pivot typed-properties are used to show all the
other entities that mention the same phone number, email address or name as the
one currently seen by the user.

class_variables.name: followthemoney.types.common.PropertyType.max_length
documentation:
The maximum length of a single value of this type. This is used to warn when
adding individual values that may be malformed or too long to be stored in
downstream databases with fixed column lengths. The unit is unicode codepoints
(not bytes), the output of Python len().

class_variables.name: followthemoney.types.common.PropertyType.total_size
documentation:
Some types have overall size limitations in place in order to avoid generating
entities that are very large (upstream ElasticSearch has a 100MB document limit).
Once the total size of all properties of this type has exceed the given limit,
an entity will refuse to add further values.
Base class for all property types.

classes.name: followthemoney.types.common.EnumType

methods.name: followthemoney.types.common.EnumType.names
documentation:
Return a mapping from property values to their labels in the current
locale.

methods.name: followthemoney.types.common.EnumType.validate
documentation: Make sure that the given code value is one of the supported set.

methods.name: followthemoney.types.common.EnumType.clean_text
documentation:
All code values are cleaned to be lowercase and trailing whitespace is
removed.

methods.name: followthemoney.types.common.EnumType.caption
documentation: Given a code value, return the label that should be shown to a user.

methods.name: followthemoney.types.common.EnumType.to_dict
documentation: When serialising the model to JSON, include all values.

Enumerated type properties are used for types which have a defined set
of possible values, like languages and countries.

classes.name: followthemoney.types.address.AddressType

methods.name: followthemoney.types.address.AddressType.clean_text
documentation: Basic clean-up.

A geographic address used to describe a location of a residence or post
box. There is no specified order for the sub-parts of an address (e.g. street,
city, postal code), and we should consider introducing an Address schema type
to retain fidelity in cases where address parts are specified.

classes.name: followthemoney.types.topic.TopicType
documentation:
Topics define a controlled vocabulary of terms applicable to some
entities, such as companies and people. They describe categories of
journalistic interest which may apply to the given entity, for example
if a given person is a criminal or a politician.

Besides the informative value, topics are ultimately supposed to bear
fruits in the context of graph-based data analysis, where they would
enable queries such as _find all paths between a government procurement
award and a politician_.

classes.name: followthemoney.types.checksum.ChecksumType
documentation:
Content hashes calculated using SHA1. Checksum references are used by
document-typed entities in Aleph to refer to raw data in the archive
(e.g. the document from which the entity is extracted).

Unfortunately, this has some security implications: in order to avoid people
getting access to documents for which they know the checksum, properties
of this type are scrubbed when submitted via the normal API. Checksums can only
be defined by uploading a document to be ingested.

classes.name: followthemoney.types.date.DateType

methods.name: followthemoney.types.date.DateType.validate
documentation: Check if a thing is a valid date.

methods.name: followthemoney.types.date.DateType.clean_text
documentation: The classic: date parsing, every which way.

A date or time stamp. This is based on ISO 8601, but meant to allow for different
degrees of precision by specifying a prefix. This means that `2021`, `2021-02`,
`2021-02-16`, `2021-02-16T21`, `2021-02-16T21:48` and `2021-02-16T21:48:52`
are all valid values, with an implied precision.

The timezone is always expected to be UTC and cannot be specified otherwise. There is
no support for calendar weeks (`2021-W7`) and date ranges (`2021-2024`).

classes.name: followthemoney.types.mimetype.MimeType
documentation:
A MIME media type are a specification of a content type on a network.
Each MIME type is assigned by IANA and consists of two parts: the type
and sub-type. Common examples are: `text/plain`, `application/json` and
`application/pdf`.

MIME type properties do not contain parameters as used in HTTP headers,
like `charset=UTF-8`.

classes.name: followthemoney.types.iban.IbanType

methods.name: followthemoney.types.iban.IbanType.clean_text
documentation:
Create a more clean, but still user-facing version of an
instance of the type.

An international bank account number, as defined in ISO 13616. IBANs are
managed by SWIFT used in the European SEPA payment system.

A notable aspect of IBANs is that they share a country prefix and validation
mechanism, but the specific length of an IBAN is dependent on the country
code defined in the first two characters: `NO8330001234567` and
`CY21002001950000357001234567` are both valid values.

classes.name: followthemoney.types.phone.PhoneType

methods.name: followthemoney.types.phone.PhoneType._parse_number
documentation:
Parse a phone number and return in international format.

If no valid phone number can be detected, None is returned. If
a country code is supplied, this will be used to infer the
prefix.

https://github.com/daviddrysdale/python-phonenumbers

A phone number in E.164 format. This means that phone numbers always
include an international country prefix (e.g. `+38760183628`). The
cleaning and validation functions for this try to be smart about by
accepting a list of countries as an argument in order to add the number
prefix.

When adding a property of this type to an entity, any country-type properties
defined for the entity are considered for validation. That means that adding a
phone number to an entity before adding a country can have a different
validation outcome from doing the two operations the other way around. Always
define the country first.
TODO: for json schema export
https://stackoverflow.com/questions/6478875/regular-expression-matching-e-164-formatted-phone-numbers

classes.name: followthemoney.model.Model

methods.name: followthemoney.model.Model.generate
documentation:
Loading the model is a weird process because the schemata reference
each other in complex ways, so the generation process cannot be fully
run as schemata are being instantiated. Hence this process needs to be
called once all schemata are loaded to finalise dereferencing the
schemata.

methods.name: followthemoney.model.Model.get
documentation:
Get a schema object based on a schema name. If the input is already
a schema object, it will just be returned.

methods.name: followthemoney.model.Model.get_qname
documentation: Get a property object based on a qualified name (i.e. schema:property).

methods.name: followthemoney.model.Model.__getitem__
documentation: Same as get(), but throws an exception when the given name does not exist.

methods.name: followthemoney.model.Model.get_type_schemata
documentation: Return all the schemata which have a property of the given type.

methods.name: followthemoney.model.Model.make_mapping
documentation: Parse a mapping that applies (tabular) source data to the model.

methods.name: followthemoney.model.Model.map_entities
documentation: Given a mapping, yield a series of entities from the data source.

methods.name: followthemoney.model.Model.common_schema
documentation:
Select the most narrow of two schemata.

When indexing data from a dataset, an entity may be declared as a
LegalEntity in one query, and as a Person in another. This function
will select the most specific of two schemata offered. In the example,
that would be Person.

methods.name: followthemoney.model.Model.make_entity
documentation: Instantiate an empty entity proxy of the given schema type.

methods.name: followthemoney.model.Model.get_proxy
documentation:
Create an entity proxy to reflect the entity data in the given
dictionary. If `cleaned` is disabled, all property values are
fully re-validated and normalised. Use this if handling input data
from an untrusted source.

methods.name: followthemoney.model.Model.to_dict
documentation: Return metadata for all schemata and properties, in a serializable form.

methods.name: followthemoney.model.Model.__iter__
documentation: Iterate across all schemata.

A collection of all the schemata available in followthemoney. The model
provides some helper functions to find schemata, properties or to instantiate
entity proxies based on the schema metadata.

classes.name: followthemoney.exc.FollowTheMoneyException
documentation: Catch-all exception for errors emitted by this library.

classes.name: followthemoney.exc.InvalidData
documentation: Schema validation errors will be caught by the API.

classes.name: followthemoney.exc.InvalidModel
documentation: The schema model is not defined correctly.

classes.name: followthemoney.exc.InvalidMapping
documentation: A data mapping was invalid.

functions.name: followthemoney.util.key_bytes
documentation: Convert the given data to a value appropriate for hashing.

functions.name: followthemoney.util.join_text
documentation: Join all the non-null arguments using sep.

functions.name: followthemoney.util.get_entity_id
documentation: Given an entity-ish object, try to get the ID.

functions.name: followthemoney.util.merge_context
documentation:
When merging two entities, make lists of all the duplicate context
keys.

functions.name: followthemoney.cli.aggregate.sorted_aggregate
documentation:
Aggregate entities based on the premise that the fragments in the source
stream are sorted by their ID.

functions.name: followthemoney.cli.util.path_writer
documentation: Open a file for writing binary content, or use stdout.

functions.name: followthemoney.cli.util.load_mapping_file
documentation: Load a YAML (or JSON) bulk load mapping file.

functions.name: followthemoney.cli.util.resolve_includes
documentation:
Handle include statements in the graph configuration file.

This allows the YAML graph configuration to be broken into
multiple smaller fragments that are easier to maintain.

classes.name: followthemoney.schema.Schema

methods.name: followthemoney.schema.Schema.generate
documentation:
While loading the schema, this function will validate and
load the hierarchy, properties, and flags of the definition.

methods.name: followthemoney.schema.Schema.label
documentation: User-facing name of the schema.

methods.name: followthemoney.schema.Schema.plural
documentation: Name of the schema to be used in plural constructions.

methods.name: followthemoney.schema.Schema.description
documentation: A longer description of the semantics of the schema.

methods.name: followthemoney.schema.Schema.edge_label
documentation: Description label for edges derived from entities of this schema.

methods.name: followthemoney.schema.Schema.source_prop
documentation: The entity property to be used as an edge source.

methods.name: followthemoney.schema.Schema.target_prop
documentation: The entity property to be used as an edge target.

methods.name: followthemoney.schema.Schema.temporal_start_props
documentation:
The entity properties to be used as the start when representing the entity
in a timeline.

methods.name: followthemoney.schema.Schema.temporal_end_props
documentation:
The entity properties to be used as the end when representing the entity
in a timeline.

methods.name: followthemoney.schema.Schema.sorted_properties
documentation:
All properties of the schema in the order in which they should be shown
to the user (alphabetically, with captions and featured properties first).

methods.name: followthemoney.schema.Schema.matchable_schemata
documentation:
Return the set of schemata to which it makes sense to compare with this
schema. For example, it makes sense to compare a legal entity with a company,
but it does not make sense to compare a car and a person.

methods.name: followthemoney.schema.Schema.can_match
documentation: Check if an schema can match with another schema.

methods.name: followthemoney.schema.Schema.is_a
documentation:
Check if the schema or one of its parents is the same as the given
candidate `other`.

methods.name: followthemoney.schema.Schema.get
documentation: Retrieve a property defined for this schema by its name.

methods.name: followthemoney.schema.Schema.validate
documentation:
Validate a dictionary against the given schema.
This will also drop keys which are not valid as properties.

methods.name: followthemoney.schema.Schema.to_dict
documentation: Return schema metadata, including all properties, in a serializable form.

methods.name: followthemoney.schema.Schema.__eq__
documentation: Compare two schemata (via hash).

A type definition for a class of entities that have certain properties.

Schemata are arranged in a multi-rooted hierarchy: each schema can have multiple
parent schemata from which it inherits all of their properties. A schema can also
have descendant child schemata, which, in turn, add further properties. Schemata
are usually accessed via the model, which holds all available definitions.

classes.name: followthemoney.mapping.sql.QueryTable
documentation: A table to be joined in.

classes.name: followthemoney.mapping.sql.SQLSource

methods.name: followthemoney.mapping.sql.SQLSource.records
documentation: Compose the actual query and return an iterator of `Record`.
Query mapper for loading data from a SQL query.

modules.name: followthemoney.graph
documentation:
Converting FtM data to a property graph data model.

This module provides an abstract data object that represents a property
graph. This is used by the exporter modules to convert data
to a specific output format, like Cypher or NetworkX.

classes.name: followthemoney.graph.Node

methods.name: followthemoney.graph.Node.is_entity
documentation:
Check to see if the node represents an entity. If this is false, the
node represents a non-entity property value that has been reified, like
a phone number or a name.

methods.name: followthemoney.graph.Node.caption
documentation: A user-facing label for the current node.

methods.name: followthemoney.graph.Node.to_dict
documentation: Return a simple dictionary to reflect this graph node.

methods.name: followthemoney.graph.Node.from_proxy
documentation:
For a given {class}`~followthemoney.proxy.EntityProxy`, return a node
based on the entity.

A node represents either an entity that can be rendered as a
node in a graph, or as a re-ified value, like a name, email
address or phone number.

classes.name: followthemoney.graph.Edge

methods.name: followthemoney.graph.Edge.source
documentation: The graph node from which the edge originates.

methods.name: followthemoney.graph.Edge.source_prop
documentation: Get the entity property originating this edge.

methods.name: followthemoney.graph.Edge.target
documentation: The graph node to which the edge points.

methods.name: followthemoney.graph.Edge.target_prop
documentation: Get the entity property originating this edge.

methods.name: followthemoney.graph.Edge.type_name
documentation:
Return a machine-readable description of the type of the edge.
This is either a property name or a schema name.
A link between two nodes.

classes.name: followthemoney.graph.Graph

methods.name: followthemoney.graph.Graph.flush
documentation: Remove all nodes, edges and proxies from the graph.

methods.name: followthemoney.graph.Graph.queue
documentation: Register a reference to an entity in the graph.

methods.name: followthemoney.graph.Graph.queued
documentation:
Return a list of all the entities which are referenced from the graph
but that haven't been loaded yet. This can be used to get a list of
entities that should be included to expand the whole graph by one degree.

methods.name: followthemoney.graph.Graph._add_node
documentation: Derive a node and its value edges from the given proxy.

methods.name: followthemoney.graph.Graph.add
documentation:
Add an {class}`~followthemoney.proxy.EntityProxy` to the graph and make
it either a {class}`~followthemoney.graph.Node` or an
{class}`~followthemoney.graph.Edge`.

methods.name: followthemoney.graph.Graph.iternodes
documentation: Iterate all {class}`nodes <followthemoney.graph.Node>` in the graph.

methods.name: followthemoney.graph.Graph.iteredges
documentation: Iterate all {class}`edges <followthemoney.graph.Edge>` in the graph.

methods.name: followthemoney.graph.Graph.get_outbound
documentation: Get all edges pointed out from the given node.

methods.name: followthemoney.graph.Graph.get_inbound
documentation: Get all edges pointed at the given node.

methods.name: followthemoney.graph.Graph.to_dict
documentation: Return a dictionary with the graph nodes and edges.

A set of nodes and edges, derived from entities and their properties.
This represents an alternative interpretation of FtM data as a property
graph.

This class is meant to be extensible in order to support additional
backends, like Aleph.

classes.name: followthemoney.mapping.csv.CSVSource

methods.name: followthemoney.mapping.csv.CSVSource.records
documentation: Iterate through the table applying filters on-the-go.
Special case for entity loading directly from a CSV URL

methods.name: followthemoney.mapping.entity.EntityMapping.compute_key
documentation: Generate a key for this entity, based on the given fields.

classes.name: followthemoney.mapping.property.PropertyMapping
documentation:
Map values from a given record (e.g. a CSV row or SQL result) to the
schema form.

modules.name: followthemoney.rdf
documentation: This module serves exclusively to mitigate the type checking clusterfuck
that is rdflib 6.0.

functions.name: followthemoney.offshore.offshore_from_jurisdiction
documentation:
Tag organizations linked to a well-known offshore jurisdiction as
offshores automatically. Complete generalization, use only in experiments.

functions.name: followthemoney.compare.compare_scores
documentation: Compare two entities and return a match score for each property.

functions.name: followthemoney.compare.compare
documentation: Compare two entities and return a match score.

functions.name: followthemoney.compare._normalize_names
documentation:
Generate a sequence of comparable names for an entity. This also
generates a `fingerprint`, i.e. a version of the name where all tokens
are sorted alphabetically, and some parts, such as company suffixes,
have been removed.

modules.name: followthemoney.namespace
documentation:
*We like our abstractions like our offshore banks: leaky.*

Entity ID namespaces are a security mechanism related to the Aleph search index.

Aleph allows the user (via mappings or the API) to create arbitrary entity IDs.
Entity IDs that are controlled by the user and not the system are unusual.
However, this makes it possible to generate bulk data outside Aleph,
and then load entities into the system as a continuous {ref}`streams`.

The problem is that having user controlled entity IDs increases the chance
of conflict in the search index.

Namespacing works around this by making each entity ID consist of two parts:
one controlled by the client, the other controlled by the system. The second
part of the ID is called its `signature`:

```
entity_id.a40a29300ac6bb79dd2f911e77bbda7a3b502126
```

The signature is generated as `hmac(entity_id, dataset_id)`. This guarantees
that the combined ID is specific to a dataset, without needing an (expensive)
index look up of each ID first. It can also be generated on the client or
the server without compromising isolation.

classes.name: followthemoney.namespace.Namespace

methods.name: followthemoney.namespace.Namespace.parse
documentation:
Split up an entity ID into the plain ID and the namespace
signature. If either part is missing, return None instead.

methods.name: followthemoney.namespace.Namespace.signature
documentation: Generate a namespace-specific signature.

methods.name: followthemoney.namespace.Namespace.sign
documentation:
Apply a namespace signature to an entity ID, removing any
previous namespace marker.

methods.name: followthemoney.namespace.Namespace.verify
documentation: Check if the signature matches the current namespace.

methods.name: followthemoney.namespace.Namespace.apply
documentation:
Rewrite an entity proxy so all IDs mentioned are limited to
the namespace.

Namespaces are used to partition entity IDs into different units,
which traditionally represent a dataset, collection or source.

See module docstring for details.

classes.name: followthemoney.export.graph.GraphExporter
documentation:
Base functions for exporting a property graph from a stream
of entities.

classes.name: followthemoney.export.graph.NXGraphExporter

methods.name: followthemoney.export.graph.NXGraphExporter.finalize_graph
documentation: Convert from FtM graph model to NetworkX directed graph.

Write to NetworkX data structure, which in turn can be exported
to the file formats for Gephi (GEXF) and D3.

classes.name: followthemoney.export.neo4j.CypherGraphExporter

methods.name: followthemoney.export.neo4j.CypherGraphExporter.__init__
documentation: https://www.opencypher.org/
MATCH (n) DETACH DELETE n;

methods.name: followthemoney.export.neo4j.CypherGraphExporter.write_graph
documentation: Export queries for each graph element.

Cypher query format, used for import to Neo4J. This is a bit like
writing SQL with individual statements - so for large datasets it
might be a better idea to do a CSV-based import.

classes.name: followthemoney.property.Property

methods.name: followthemoney.property.Property.generate
documentation:
Setup method used when loading the model in order to build out the reverse
links of the property.

methods.name: followthemoney.property.Property.label
documentation: User-facing title for this property.

methods.name: followthemoney.property.Property.description
documentation: A longer description of the semantics of this property.

methods.name: followthemoney.property.Property.specificity
documentation: Return a measure of how precise the given value is.

methods.name: followthemoney.property.Property.validate
documentation:
Validate that the data should be stored.

Since the types system doesn't really have validation, this currently
tries to normalize the value to see if it passes strict parsing.

methods.name: followthemoney.property.Property.to_dict
documentation: Return property metadata in a serializable form.

A definition of a value-holding field on a schema. Properties define
the field type and other possible constraints. They also serve as entity
to entity references.

classes.name: followthemoney.proxy.EntityProxy

methods.name: followthemoney.proxy.EntityProxy.make_id
documentation:
Generate a (hopefully unique) ID for the given entity, composed
of the given components, and the {attr}`~key_prefix` defined in
the proxy.

methods.name: followthemoney.proxy.EntityProxy.get
documentation:
Get all values of a property.

```
param prop

can be given as a name or an instance of
.

param quiet

a reference to an non-existent property will return
an empty list instead of raising an error.

return

A list of values.
```

methods.name: followthemoney.proxy.EntityProxy.first
documentation:
Get only the first value set for the property.

```
param prop

can be given as a name or an instance of
.

param quiet

a reference to an non-existent property will return
an empty list instead of raising an error.

return

A value, or None.
```

methods.name: followthemoney.proxy.EntityProxy.has
documentation:
Check to see if the given property has at least one value set.

```
param prop

can be given as a name or an instance of
.

param quiet

a reference to an non-existent property will return
an empty list instead of raising an error.

return

a boolean.
```

methods.name: followthemoney.proxy.EntityProxy.add
documentation:
Add the given value(s) to the property if they are valid for
the type of the property.

```
param prop

can be given as a name or an instance of
.

param values

either a single value, or a list of values to be added.

param cleaned

should the data be normalised before adding it.

param quiet

a reference to an non-existent property will return
an empty list instead of raising an error.

param fuzzy

when normalising the data, should fuzzy matching be allowed.

param format

when normalising the data, formatting for a date.
```

methods.name: followthemoney.proxy.EntityProxy.unsafe_add
documentation:
A version of `add()` to be used only in type-checking code. This accepts
only a single value, and performs input cleaning on the premise that the
value is already valid unicode. Returns the value that has been added.

methods.name: followthemoney.proxy.EntityProxy.set
documentation:
Replace the values of the property with the given value(s).

```
param prop

can be given as a name or an instance of
.

param values

either a single value, or a list of values to be added.

param cleaned

should the data be normalised before adding it.

param quiet

a reference to an non-existent property will return
an empty list instead of raising an error.
```

methods.name: followthemoney.proxy.EntityProxy.pop
documentation:
Remove all the values from the given property and return them.

```
param prop

can be given as a name or an instance of
.

param quiet

a reference to an non-existent property will return
an empty list instead of raising an error.

return

a list of values, possibly empty.
```

methods.name: followthemoney.proxy.EntityProxy.remove
documentation:
Remove a single value from the given property. If it is not there,
no action takes place.

```
param prop

can be given as a name or an instance of
.

param value

will not be cleaned before checking.

param quiet

a reference to an non-existent property will return
an empty list instead of raising an error.
```

methods.name: followthemoney.proxy.EntityProxy.iterprops
documentation:
Iterate across all the properties for which a value is set in
the proxy (but do not return their values).

methods.name: followthemoney.proxy.EntityProxy.itervalues
documentation:
Iterate across all values in the proxy one by one, each given as a
tuple of the property and the value.

methods.name: followthemoney.proxy.EntityProxy.edgepairs
documentation:
Return all the possible pairs of values for the edge source and target if
the schema allows for an edge representation of the entity.

methods.name: followthemoney.proxy.EntityProxy.get_type_values
documentation:
All values of a particular type associated with a the entity. For
example, this lets you return all countries linked to an entity, rather
than manually checking each property to see if it contains countries.

```
param type

The type object to be searched.

param matchable

Whether to return only property values marked as matchable.
```

methods.name: followthemoney.proxy.EntityProxy.names
documentation: Get the set of all name-type values set of the entity.

methods.name: followthemoney.proxy.EntityProxy.countries
documentation: Get the set of all country-type values set of the entity.

methods.name: followthemoney.proxy.EntityProxy.temporal_start
documentation:
Get a date that can be used to represent the start of the entity in a
timeline. If there are multiple possible dates, the earliest date is
returned.

methods.name: followthemoney.proxy.EntityProxy.temporal_end
documentation:
Get a date that can be used to represent the end of the entity in a timeline.
If therer are multiple possible dates, the latest date is returned.

methods.name: followthemoney.proxy.EntityProxy.get_type_inverted
documentation:
Return all the values of the entity arranged into a mapping with the
group name of their property type. These groups include `countries`,
`addresses`, `emails`, etc.

methods.name: followthemoney.proxy.EntityProxy.triples
documentation:
Serialise the entity into a set of RDF triple statements. The
statements include the property values, an `RDF#type` definition
that refers to the entity schema, and a `SKOS#prefLabel` with the
entity caption.

methods.name: followthemoney.proxy.EntityProxy.caption
documentation:
The user-facing label to be used for this entity. This checks a list
of properties defined by the schema (caption) and returns the first
available value. If no caption is available, return the schema label.

methods.name: followthemoney.proxy.EntityProxy.country_hints
documentation:
Some property types, such as phone numbers and IBAN codes imply a
country that may be associated with the entity. This list can be used
for a more generous matching approach than the actual country values.

methods.name: followthemoney.proxy.EntityProxy.properties
documentation: Return a mapping of the properties and set values of the entity.

methods.name: followthemoney.proxy.EntityProxy.to_dict
documentation:
Serialise the proxy into a dictionary with the defined properties, ID,
schema and any contextual values that were handed in initially. The resulting
dictionary can be used to make a new proxy, and it is commonly written to disk
or a database.

methods.name: followthemoney.proxy.EntityProxy.to_full_dict
documentation:
Return a serialised version of the entity with inverted type groups mixed
in. See {meth}`~get_type_inverted`.

methods.name: followthemoney.proxy.EntityProxy.clone
documentation: Make a deep copy of the current entity proxy.

methods.name: followthemoney.proxy.EntityProxy.merge
documentation:
Merge another entity proxy into this one. This will try and find
the common schema between both entities and then add all property
values from the other entity into this one.

methods.name: followthemoney.proxy.EntityProxy.from_dict
documentation:
Instantiate a proxy based on the given model and serialised dictionary.

Use {meth}`followthemoney.model.Model.get_proxy` instead.

A wrapper object for an entity, with utility functions for the
introspection and manipulation of its properties.

This is the main working object in the library, used to generate, validate
and emit data.
